attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the Birth Suite in active labour with her first baby at 39 weeks gestation. She has no significant medical history and has had an uncomplicated pregnancy. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She is experiencing regular painful contractions every three minutes. Vaginal examination reveals that her cervix is 5 cm dilated with a well-applied fetal head. Intermittent auscultation of the fetal heart is reassuring. She is interested in using nitrous oxide for pain management.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most correct regarding nitrous oxide use in labour?
options: Nitrous oxide needs to be used cautiously close to delivery due to risk of neonatal respiratory depression
Nitrous oxide can result in reduced sensation and mobility of lower limbs
Nitrous oxide use needs to be monitored closely due to the risk of overdose
Nitrous oxide requires the patient to hold and control the delivery of analgesia and overdose is not possible
Nitrous oxide is the most appropriate choice when a patient requires analgesia over a long period of time
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a woman in active labour requesting pain relief. Nitrous oxide (50% nitrous oxide/50% oxygen mixture) is a safe and appropriate first-line analgesic option that can be self-administered through a demand valve system.

Nitrous oxide requires the patient to hold and control the delivery of analgesia through a demand valve system. This means that if the woman becomes drowsy, she will naturally drop the mouthpiece, making overdose virtually impossible. The rapid onset and offset of action (30-50 seconds) allows women to use it only during contractions.

Nitrous oxide does not need to be used cautiously close to delivery as it is rapidly eliminated from both maternal and fetal circulation. Unlike opioids, there is no significant risk of neonatal respiratory depression.

Nitrous oxide does not affect sensation or mobility of the lower limbs. This is in contrast to epidural analgesia. Women using nitrous oxide can remain mobile during labour, which many find beneficial.

Close monitoring for overdose is not required due to the self-limiting nature of the delivery system. The main side effects are nausea, dizziness, and drowsiness, which quickly resolve when use is discontinued.

Nitrous oxide may not be the most appropriate choice for prolonged analgesia as some women find it provides insufficient pain relief, particularly in advanced labour. Other options like epidural analgesia might be more suitable for prolonged pain relief.

The key advantages of nitrous oxide include its safety profile, ease of administration, rapid onset/offset, and the fact that it allows women to remain mobile during labour. It can be particularly useful during the first stage of labour and can be combined with other pain relief methods if needed.
learningPoints: Nitrous oxide is a safe and effective first-line analgesic option in active labour that allows women to remain mobile.
Nitrous oxide is self-administered with a demand valve system making overdose virtually impossible.
Nitrous oxide has rapid onset and offset with minimal side effects for mother and baby.
references: Smith A, Laflamme E, Komanecky C. Pain management in labor. Am Fam Physician. 2021;103(6):355-364.
